topic: A college student discovers their roommate has been reading their journal
raw_output:
[story 1]

The scent of old paper and faint vanilla, usually comforting in our shared room, suddenly felt suffocating. I walked in to find Mark perched on his desk chair, a book open in his lap. It wasn't just any book; it was my journal. My breath hitched in my throat, a cold knot tightening in my stomach. I stared, unable to move, the silence of the room amplifying the frantic drumming in my ears. When he looked up, his expression was unreadable, a strange mixture of nonchalance and something else—something knowing. "Found something interesting?" he asked, his voice level, which only made my skin prickle. I mumbled something incoherent, backing away slowly, my eyes fixed on the closed book as if it held a secret capable of shattering the floorboards.

[story 2]

Every time I entered our room, a tremor ran through me. It started subtly—a slight hesitation before I reached the door, a quick glance toward the desk. But then I saw it. My meticulously kept journal was open, the ink still wet on the page, the handwriting undeniably mine, interspersed with his own annotations in the margins. My hands began to shake as I approached. I stood frozen by the doorway, my knuckles white where I gripped the frame. I could feel the heat rising in my cheeks, a desperate urge to flee. When he finally noticed my paralysis, he closed the book with deliberate slowness, a small, almost imperceptible smile playing on his lips. "Just catching up on some thoughts," he murmured, and the casualness of the statement felt like a physical blow.

[story 3]

I found the evidence tucked beneath a pile of textbooks: my private reflections, laid out like an offering on the shared surface. A wave of nausea washed over me, making the fluorescent lights of the dorm room seem painfully bright. I didn't speak; I just stood there, my gaze locked on the page. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic rhythm that seemed too loud for the quiet space. When my roommate finally entered, carrying a stack of laundry, he stopped dead in his tracks. The air thickened instantly. He looked from the journal to my face, and for a long moment, nothing happened. Then, he cleared his throat, a dry, raspy sound, and asked, "What are you reading?" The question hung between us, heavy and charged, and I could only manage a shallow, shaky breath.
